metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py: 3b30-2168-ba16-41fc & c23c-c32b-982c-4196 (no ordering edge)
src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py: 9597-1b85-d2ab-4eed & c23c-c32b-982c-4196 (no ordering edge)
findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (or expand-contract sequence with a deprecation phase) for the replacement of existing `code_health` readers, covering both the zeros→Unavailable behavioral change and the removal of `.github/module-size-limit.txt` enforcement.
evidence: 'Hard fallback contract: if no analyzer is configured for the client's language, emit a genuine Unavailable("no analyzer configured for <lang>") — NEVER scan a hardcoded path, NEVER fabricate zeros'
The plan removes `.github/module-size-limit.txt` and hardcoded `src/rebar/**/*.py` paths with no deprecation/dual-emit phase described
No rollback path is mentioned anywhere in the plan — the old readers are simply replaced
findings.finding: The plan replaces existing `code_health` metric readers with behavioral changes (zeros→Unavailable, removed `.github/module-size-limit.txt` enforcement) but provides no expand-contract sequencing or explicit rollback/back-out plan for consumers currently relying on the old behavior.
findings.location: Design section — Hard fallback contract; Acceptance criteria (no rollback AC present)
scenarios: A client CI pipeline that parses `code_health` metric results and alerts on zero values will silently stop alerting after upgrade
A governance workflow enforcing module-size caps via the old mechanism loses enforcement with no warning or migration guide
If the new analyzer stack has a bug at rollout, there is no described path to revert to the previous readers
findings.suggested_fix: Add a plan section 'Migration & Rollback' that: (1) describes a dual-emit deprecation phase where the old readers emit a deprecation warning alongside the new Unavailable signal; (2) specifies the rollback procedure (e.g., re-enable old readers via a feature flag or pinned version); (3) identifies which CI/governance artifacts referencing module-size-limit.txt must be updated before the old path is removed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a migration/deprecation note to the plan that explicitly acknowledges callers depending on the old `code_health` return shape (fabricated zeros at confidence=high) and describes how they should adapt when the behavior changes to `Unavailable`.
evidence: Plan states old behavior: 'fabricate authoritative-labeled zeros (scan an absent path -> {count:0,max_loc:0} at confidence=high) or leak a raw FileNotFoundError'
Plan's new contract: 'emit a genuine Unavailable("no analyzer configured for <lang>") — NEVER scan a hardcoded path, NEVER fabricate zeros'
No section in the plan addresses consumers of the old behavior or provides a migration/deprecation notice
findings.finding: The plan changes existing behavior — `code_health` metrics that previously returned fabricated-zero data at confidence=high (or leaked exceptions) will now return `Unavailable` — but does not acknowledge downstream callers or consumers that depend on the old return shape. Any CI gates, dashboards, or downstream code that branches on confidence=high or treats `{count:0, max_loc:0}` as a passing signal will silently start receiving `Unavailable` instead, breaking those callers without a migration note.
findings.location: Design — Hard fallback contract; Acceptance criteria AC4
scenarios: A CI pipeline that currently passes because module_size_distribution returns {count:0, max_loc:0} at confidence=high for a Go or Ruby project (below any size cap) will receive Unavailable after the change and may error or produce unexpected output, depending on how the caller handles that signal.

A dashboard or reporting consumer that reads code_health data and displays it assuming a numeric result will encounter Unavailable and may crash or display nothing, with no warning from the plan that this transition is coming.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Migration impact' subsection under 'Design' that: (1) names the affected metrics (`module_size_distribution`, `cap_change_events`); (2) states the old return shape and the new one; (3) recommends that callers check for `Unavailable` before consuming the result; (4) notes whether a version bump or changelog entry will accompany this change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a formal depends_on or relates_to edge from a9dd to ticket 121b, and add a fallback clause: 'if 121b is absent, create tests/scripts/test_metrics_docs.py from scratch with the 121b assertions plus the new ones.'
evidence: a9dd Approach: 'EXTEND the existing tests/scripts/test_metrics_docs.py (created by ticket 121b) rather than adding a parallel test.'
121b is not in the sibling roster and has no depends_on edge recorded in a9dd's dependency list.
a9dd Dependencies: '0734 (content prerequisite; relates_to edge recorded)' — 121b absent.
findings.finding: a9dd depends on 'tests/scripts/test_metrics_docs.py (created by ticket 121b)' — a ticket outside the sibling roster with no recorded depends_on edge. If 121b is not yet landed when a9dd executes, the file to be 'extended' does not exist, and a9dd's approach of adding a new test assertion to that file fails at implementation time.
findings.location: child a9dd-041f-de89-48c5
scenarios: a9dd is implemented before 121b lands; tests/scripts/test_metrics_docs.py does not exist; the implementer must create the file from scratch (contradicting the 'extend' framing) or block on an untracked dependency.
The 121b ticket is cancelled or renamed; a9dd's approach becomes invalid with no fallback specified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to a9dd: 'Dependencies: 0734, 121b (creates the base test file; if absent, a9dd creates it). Files: tests/scripts/test_metrics_docs.py (create or extend).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the units and aggregation semantics of each `analyze()` return field (e.g., `loc: int total non-blank non-comment lines`, `complexity: float avg CCN per function from lizard; scc value used only when lizard unavailable`, `duplication: float 0–1 duplicate-line ratio from jscpd`).
evidence: Plan states the interface returns {loc, complexity, duplication} but gives no type, unit, or aggregation rule.
Recommended stack includes both scc ('approx complexity') and lizard ('TRUE cyclomatic complexity CCN') for complexity — two tools, one output field, no reconciliation rule stated.
findings.finding: The `analyze()` return schema `{loc, complexity, duplication}` has no stated units, granularity, or aggregation protocol. scc gives approximate complexity per-file, lizard gives true CCN per-function/file — when both are used, it is unspecified which value is authoritative, whether they are summed/averaged/max'd, or what the scalar type of each field is.
findings.location: Design section — 'Define an analyzer interface: analyze(repo_root, languages) -> {loc, complexity, duplication}'
scenarios: Executor implements two tools contributing to 'complexity' and must choose arbitrarily how to combine or prioritize them.
Client consuming the metric gets different numbers depending on which tool was run, with no documented semantics.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection 'Analyzer output contract' listing each field with its type, unit (e.g. lines, CCN score, percentage), aggregation level (repo-level vs. per-file), and which tool is authoritative when multiple tools produce the same signal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the config schema (file, section, and key names) for at minimum: `scan_root`, `language`, `size_cap_loc`, and `analyzer_tool`; a minimal example snippet is sufficient.
evidence: Plan references 'per-project config' and 'thresholds' without naming a config file (e.g., pyproject.toml [tool.rebar], rebar.toml, .rebar/config.yaml), schema keys, or how rebar discovers project-level config.
AC3 references that 'scan root + size policy are per-project config' but no config key name is given.
findings.finding: The per-project config is mentioned ('Per-project config selects tool + thresholds') but no schema, file format, or location is specified. An executor cannot implement configuration loading without inventing these details.
findings.location: Design section — 'Per-project config selects tool + thresholds; the rebar module-size CAP becomes one optional policy layered on the generic LOC signal'
scenarios: Executor writes config loading against a schema they invented; a second executor writes a consumer expecting different keys — incompatible config files in the wild.
User tries to configure the analyzer but no documented config key exists, so they inspect source code.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Configuration' subsection with a minimal TOML/YAML example showing at least `[rebar.metrics]` with keys like `scan_root`, `languages`, and `size_cap_loc`, plus a note on discovery order (e.g., pyproject.toml > rebar.toml > env var).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC1 with an observable assertion: e.g., 'integration tests for a Python repo fixture and a Go repo fixture assert `loc > 0`, `complexity > 0`, and `duplication` is a float in [0,1]; CI runs both test suites green'.
evidence: 'computed' does not define a passing condition — any non-exception result could satisfy it.
No test fixture, expected output range, or CI command is referenced.
The 'e.g. Go or TS' phrasing is a minor defaultable gap on language choice, but the absence of any success assertion is the main F1 issue.
findings.finding: AC1 says 'code_health metrics computed via the analyzer stack for a Python client AND at least one non-Python client' but does not state what observable output or assertion constitutes 'computed correctly'. There is no reference to test fixtures, expected value ranges, or a specific integration test command, making the criterion effort-framed ('we ran the tools') rather than outcome-observable ('the metric value equals X for fixture Y').
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — AC1: '`code_health` metrics computed via the analyzer stack (scc/lizard/jscpd) for a Python client AND at least one non-Python client (e.g. Go or TS).'
scenarios: An executor returns `{loc: 0, complexity: 0, duplication: 0.0}` from a misconfigured tool path; the AC passes trivially.
Reviewer cannot distinguish a correct implementation from a stub that returns plausible-looking zeros.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC1: 'verified by integration tests against a small Python fixture repo and a small Go fixture repo (committed under tests/fixtures/), asserting loc > 0, 0 ≤ duplication ≤ 1, and complexity > 0 for each.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether lizard and jscpd must be *reclassified* from their current dep status to the `[metrics]` optional extra (and add that as explicit described work), or confirm they are already correctly isolated as non-core deps.
evidence: 'lizard (MIT, Python)... Already a rebar dev/backfill dep.'
'jscpd (MIT)... Already a rebar dep.'
AC#2: 'Zero new HARD deps in rebar core; analyzer + tools live behind pip install rebar[metrics]; core-only install reports code_health as Unavailable'
Design: 'Package tools + analyzer under an optional [metrics] extra; core install stays dependency-free'
findings.finding: The 'Recommended stack' section describes lizard as 'Already a rebar dev/backfill dep' and jscpd as 'Already a rebar dep,' implying they are currently in the dependency graph, yet AC#2 and the Design assert that 'analyzer + tools live behind pip install rebar[metrics]' and 'core install stays dependency-free.' These statements conflict: if lizard and jscpd are already deps, either (a) they need explicit reclassification to the [metrics] optional group (additional work not described), or (b) the AC#2 zero-new-hard-deps claim is already satisfied trivially, making it misleading. An implementer would not know whether reclassification is in scope.
findings.location: Recommended stack section ('Already a rebar dev/backfill dep' / 'Already a rebar dep') vs AC#2 and Design packaging statement
scenarios: Implementer leaves lizard as a dev/core dep, technically satisfying existing dep status but violating the [metrics]-extra packaging intent.
Implementer reclassifies lizard/jscpd to optional extras (additional work), discovering this was implicit but unspecified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the Design section: 'lizard and jscpd are currently listed as dev/backfill deps; this work moves them to the [metrics] optional extras group so they are absent from a bare pip install rebar.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a body section (or expand Design) describing the documentation deliverable: what 'reuse-surface' refers to, where the user-guide entry lives, and the minimum content required (extra installation, per-project config schema, fallback behavior).
evidence: AC#6: 'Docs (reuse-surface, user-guide) describe the extra, config, and the fallback contract.'
ABSENCE: No section in Goal, Why, Design, or Out-of-scope describes documentation deliverables, doc location, or doc content requirements.
findings.finding: AC#6 ('Docs (reuse-surface, user-guide) describe the extra, config, and the fallback contract') has no corresponding coverage in the plan body — no section describes what documentation work is needed, what 'reuse-surface' means, where the user-guide lives, or what the docs must contain. This is an orphan criterion with no described deliverable to implement against.
findings.location: AC#6 vs plan body (Goal, Why, Design sections)
scenarios: Implementer skips or minimally addresses docs because no body section specifies what is required, then AC#6 fails review.
'reuse-surface' is undefined — implementer cannot know which surface to document.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion verifying the analyzer interface contract (signature, return shape `{loc, complexity, duplication}`, and determinism) so it is explicitly testable.
evidence: Design: 'Define an analyzer interface: analyze(repo_root, languages) -> {loc, complexity, duplication}, shelling out to the configured tool(s); deterministic, no LLM.'
ABSENCE: No AC references the interface, its signature, determinism property, or shell-out behavior.
findings.finding: The Design section specifies a concrete analyzer interface ('analyze(repo_root, languages) -> {loc, complexity, duplication}') as a described deliverable, but no acceptance criterion verifies that this interface is implemented, testable, or conforms to the specified signature. This is orphan described work — the interface contract is central to portability yet uncovered by any AC.
findings.location: Design section ('Define an analyzer interface: analyze(repo_root, languages) -> {loc, complexity, duplication}')
scenarios: Implementer ships a working but different interface (e.g., different return shape) and all ACs pass because none test the interface contract.
The 'deterministic' property and 'no LLM' constraint go unverified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'The analyzer exposes an `analyze(repo_root, languages)` interface returning `{loc, complexity, duplication}`; calling it twice on the same input returns identical results (deterministic).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the docs AC to explicitly cover (a) auditing and updating/removing existing references to `src/rebar/**/*.py` and `.github/module-size-limit.txt`, and (b) producing an ADR capturing the polyglot tool-stack decision and the rationale for rejecting rust-code-analysis.
evidence: 'Docs (reuse-surface, user-guide) describe the extra, config, and the fallback contract' — covers new docs only
No mention of auditing/updating existing docs that reference `src/rebar/**/*.py` or `.github/module-size-limit.txt`
No ADR mentioned for adopting scc/lizard/jscpd and retiring the Python-only readers — a notable architectural decision
findings.finding: The plan does not address stale references in existing documentation, CI workflows, or governance artifacts that reference the hardcoded paths (`src/rebar/**/*.py`, `.github/module-size-limit.txt`) being removed, nor does it include an ADR for the architectural decision to adopt the polyglot stack.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — Docs AC; 'No hardcoded src/rebar / .github/module-size-limit.txt' AC
scenarios: A developer reads the existing user guide referencing module-size-limit.txt governance and follows stale instructions after the file is removed
An ADR is absent, leaving the rationale for tool selection (e.g., why not rust-code-analysis) undocumented for future maintainers
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Docs AC: 'Audit existing docs and CI for references to `src/rebar/**/*.py` and `.github/module-size-limit.txt`; update or retire them. Produce an ADR documenting the tool-stack selection rationale (scc/lizard/jscpd vs. alternatives).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a spike/RED-fixture plan that: (1) invokes each tool (scc, lizard, jscpd) with the proposed flags on a known repo and asserts the output parses to the unified schema; (2) exercises a core-only install and asserts `code_health` returns `Unavailable` with the expected reason string — before full implementation begins.
evidence: 'Define an analyzer interface: analyze(repo_root, languages) -> {loc, complexity, duplication}, shelling out to the configured tool(s)' — asserted as achievable with no prototype
No spike, fixture, probe, or measurement step appears anywhere in the plan
Tool versions are pinned (scc v4.x, lizard v1.23, jscpd v5) but their CLI/output-format compatibility with the proposed unified schema is untested
'core-only install reports code_health as Unavailable with a clear reason' — the conditional-import path is asserted correct with no test fixture
findings.finding: The plan shells out to three tools (scc/lizard/jscpd) and asserts a unified `analyze() -> {loc, complexity, duplication}` schema without any spike or probe validating (a) CLI invocation flags and output formats across tool versions, (b) the mapping/normalization from each tool's differing output schemas to the unified shape, or (c) that the optional-extra conditional-import fallback correctly degrades to Unavailable in a core-only install. No empirical validation step (spike, RED fixture, prototype, benchmark) is present in the plan.
findings.location: Design section / 'Recommended default stack' section / AC line 2 (core-only install reports Unavailable)
scenarios: scc v4.x JSON output schema differs from what the adapter expects, causing silent parse failures or wrong LOC numbers
lizard and scc LOC definitions diverge, producing inconsistent metrics across runs
jscpd v5 CLI flags changed from v3/v4; shell-out silently produces no output
Core-only install doesn't correctly catch the ImportError/ModuleNotFoundError, leaking an exception instead of Unavailable
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Spike / Validation' section to the plan with two tasks: (1) a throw-away script or pytest fixture that shells out to each tool on a small fixture repo, captures output, and asserts the fields needed for the unified schema are present and correctly typed; (2) a tox/pip environment test that installs rebar WITHOUT [metrics] and asserts the Unavailable path is exercised. Gate the full implementation on spike results.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or task requiring an ADR (or AGENTS.md / internal design-doc section) capturing: the three-tool composition rationale, the `analyze()` interface contract, and the hard fallback invariants — so future contributors cannot accidentally violate them.
evidence: AC6 mentions only 'reuse-surface, user-guide' docs — no ADR, AGENTS.md, or architecture decision record is listed
The hard fallback contract ('NEVER scan a hardcoded path, NEVER fabricate zeros, NEVER leak an errno') is a non-obvious invariant that future contributors must not accidentally break
ABSENCE: no ADR or design-doc update appears anywhere in the plan or acceptance criteria
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — a polyglot analyzer abstraction shelling out to three tools with an explicit fallback contract and an optional-extra packaging boundary — but does not capture this decision in an ADR, AGENTS.md entry, or design doc update. The Acceptance Criteria only require user-facing docs (reuse-surface, user-guide), not an internal design record.
findings.location: AC line 6 ('Docs (reuse-surface, user-guide) describe the extra, config, and the fallback contract') / Design section
scenarios: A future contributor adds a new metric reader and inadvertently bypasses the fallback contract because the invariant is only documented in user-facing docs, not in the internal design record
The three-tool composition rationale (why not a single polyglot tool) is lost, leading to ad-hoc additions of new tools without understanding the interface contract
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'An ADR (or equivalent AGENTS.md section) is authored capturing: (a) tool selection rationale and alternatives considered, (b) the analyze() interface contract, (c) the hard fallback invariants (no fabricated zeros, no leaked errno, no hardcoded paths), and (d) the optional-extra packaging boundary decision.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale to the Design section explaining whether `ANALYZERS`/`analyze_or_unavailable` is the intended extension seam or whether the three adapters are intentionally hardwired; if the `analyzers` config key is meant to select tools at runtime, document how `ctx` should be hydrated from config and how `ANALYZERS` gets populated — or explicitly state it is not operative in v1 and describe what the seam actually is.
evidence: Plan: 'Per-project config selects tool + thresholds'
Plan: 'Define an analyzer interface: analyze(repo_root, languages) -> {loc, complexity, duplication}'
src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py:32 — `ANALYZERS: dict[str, Analyzer] = {}` (populated only in tests, never from config)
src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py:140-148 — `ctx` namespace omits `code_health.analyzers` entirely
src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py:187-208 — three adapters called directly, bypassing ANALYZERS dispatch
ABSENCE: no code path that reads `code_health.analyzers` and populates `ANALYZERS` or `ctx`
findings.finding: The plan states 'Per-project config selects tool + thresholds' as a design principle and defines `analyze(repo_root, languages)` as the analyzer interface, but provides no rationale for why the actual implementation takes a different (hardwired three-adapter) approach. In the codebase the `ANALYZERS` dict and `analyze_or_unavailable` dispatch path are scaffolded but not wired into the command context — `ctx` never receives `code_health.analyzers`, the `ANALYZERS` dict is never populated from config, and the three concrete adapters are called directly in `git_metrics.py`. The plan's design narrative implies the `analyzers` config key is operative for per-language tool routing; an implementer following the plan will be confused about which seam actually controls tool selection and may build the wrong wiring.
findings.location: Design section — 'Per-project config selects tool + thresholds; the rebar module-size CAP becomes one optional policy layered on the generic LOC signal'
scenarios: An implementer following the plan adds a new language adapter and registers it via `ANALYZERS` per the Protocol seam, but it is never invoked because `git_metrics.py` hardwires the three concrete adapters.
A user configures `analyzers = { go = 'scc' }` expecting tool routing, but the config field is silently ignored by the command context, giving no feedback.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Design section, add a sentence such as: 'In v1 the three adapters (scc/lizard/jscpd) are hardwired; the `analyzers` config key is metadata (for future routing) and does not currently select a different tool at runtime. The extension seam for a new adapter is adding a new module under `rebar.metrics.analyzers` and wiring a new `_structural_spec` call in `git_metrics.register()`.' This aligns the plan's narrative with the actual approach and prevents implementers from targeting the wrong seam.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (or add to ACs) a live `scc --help` and `jscpd --help` probe confirming the exact flag names (`--format json`, `--reporters json`, `--output`) and minimum supported versions (scc ≥ 4.x, jscpd ≥ 5.x), and add a runtime minimum-version check or clear version-mismatch message to the Unavailable reason string.
evidence: Plan: 'scc (MIT, Go) — LOC + fast size/DRYness + approx complexity + COCOMO. v4.x'
Plan: 'jscpd (MIT) — duplication/clone detection, 223 formats, Rabin-Karp. v5.'
scc_loc.py: command = ['scc', '--format', 'json', str(scan_root)] — flag name/value unverified against scc --help
_jscpd.py: ['jscpd', '--reporters', 'json', '--output', output_dir, str(scan_root)] — flag name and output filename 'jscpd-report.json' unverified against jscpd --help
_jscpd.py: report['statistics']['total']['clones'] / ['percentage'] — JSON path unverified against live jscpd v5 output
scc is UNRESOLVED in the installed environment (Go binary, not pip-installable); jscpd is an npm tool absent from site-packages — neither can be probed via resolve_symbol
Evidence class for scc and jscpd CLI surfaces: Partially-verified (training knowledge only, not observed)
findings.finding: The plan asserts specific CLI flag names and JSON output schemas for scc (`--format json`, `Files[].Location`, `Files[].Code`) and jscpd (`--reporters json`, `--output`, `jscpd-report.json`, `statistics.total.{clones,percentage}`) without documenting a live --help probe or spike. Both tools are external binaries not installed in the Python environment, so their flag-level behavior is Unverified in this review. A flag or schema mismatch between the assumed version and what a client has installed would produce an Unavailable result (good) but no diagnostic hint about why — and silent schema drift (e.g. scc v3→v4 field renames, or jscpd v4→v5 report-path changes) could produce invalid-JSON Unavailables that are hard to diagnose. The plan names scc v4.x and jscpd v5 but neither the plan nor the ACs call for a live integration test or minimum-version guard to close this loop.
findings.location: Recommended default stack section; Design section; scc_loc.py line 40; _jscpd.py lines 29-34
scenarios: A client installs scc v3.x where the JSON field for code lines was named differently — the parse fails with 'scc produced invalid JSON' Unavailable, giving no hint about version mismatch.

jscpd v5 changes the output filename or the --reporters flag syntax vs v3/v4 — the adapter silently returns Unavailable with 'jscpd did not produce jscpd-report.json', again giving no version guidance.
CI runs against a jscpd version where --reporters is --reporter (singular) — no error at plan-commit time, only discovered at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a version-probe step to each adapter's `analyze()`: run `scc --version` / `jscpd --version`, parse the semver, and if below the required floor emit `Unavailable(reason='scc ≥ 4.0 required, found X.Y')` rather than a confusing parse-failure message. Document the confirmed flag names from `--help` in an inline comment (e.g. `# scc v4.x: --format json produces [...] verified 2026-01`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add c23c-c32b-982c-4196 as a formal depends_on edge in a532's dependency list.
evidence: a532 Dependencies: '0734 (recorded edge); transitively 18f9/21fb/3ba0' — c23c is absent from this list.
c23c: 'The cross-language validation story a532 must depend on this integration slice.'
a532 AC: 'Python and Go interface tests assert loc, complexity, and duplication values through the real metrics entry point when tools are available.'
findings.finding: a532 does not formally list c23c as a dependency, but c23c explicitly states 'The cross-language validation story a532 must depend on this integration slice.' If a532 executes without c23c landed, complexity and duplication MetricSpecs will not be registered, and a532's ACs (which assert loc, complexity, and duplication values through the real metrics entry point) will silently test absent or nonexistent metrics.
scenarios: a532 is scheduled or executed before c23c is complete; complexity/duplication MetricSpecs do not exist yet; a532's assertions pass vacuously (Unavailable for the wrong reason) or fail noisily without a clear diagnosis.
A planner reads a532's dependency list and does not know c23c must precede it.
findings.suggested_fix: In child a532, change 'Dependencies: 0734 (recorded edge); transitively 18f9/21fb/3ba0' to 'Dependencies: c23c (primary; recorded edge), 0734 (transitive); transitively 18f9/21fb/3ba0/9597/e7a0.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add concrete ACs to c23c naming specific metric IDs (e.g. avg_cyclomatic_complexity, clone_duplication_pct), payload shapes, and proving pytest commands analogous to 0734's AC format.
evidence: c23c AC1: 'The code_health lens exposes registered complexity and duplication metrics backed by the lizard and jscpd adapters; adapter outputs are not dead code.' — no metric ID, no payload shape, no pytest command.
Parent AC1: 'code_health metrics computed via the analyzer stack (scc/lizard/jscpd) for a Python client AND at least one non-Python client.'
All other children (0734, 18f9, 3ba0, e7a0, a9dd) have explicit 'PROVE: pytest -q ...' lines for each AC.
findings.finding: c23c's acceptance criteria for registering complexity and duplication MetricSpecs are underspecified: no metric IDs are named, no payload shapes are defined, and no 'PROVE: pytest ...' commands are given — unlike every other child which provides explicit observable test commands. This leaves the completion verifier unable to confirm the parent's AC1 ('code_health metrics computed via scc/lizard/jscpd') for the complexity and duplication signals.
findings.location: child c23c-c32b-982c-4196
scenarios: c23c is closed as 'done' after wiring adapters into a private helper that is never reachable from the metrics CLI, satisfying the letter of the vague AC while the parent criterion remains unmet.
The completion verifier cannot run a deterministic command to confirm complexity and duplication metrics are registered and observable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add ACs such as: 'PROVE: pytest -q tests/unit/metrics/test_complexity_metrics.py::test_complexity_registered' verifying that named MetricSpec IDs for complexity and duplication are present in the registry and return real values (not Unavailable) when adapters are available, and Unavailable with a named reason when absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in c23c (or 0734) how the composition cache interacts with 0734's direct scc_loc.analyze call — either route 0734 through the composition layer or document that LOC-only metrics are exempt from the dedup guarantee.
evidence: 0734: '_analyzer_spec(id, fn): its compute(ctx) obtains the aggregate LOC by calling scc_loc.analyze(ctx.repo_root, ctx.scan_roots) ... using the LOC analyzer directly (vs per-language resolve) matches the polyglot aggregate nature of size metrics.'
c23c: 'a code-health composition seam that invokes each configured signal producer at most once per metrics evaluation context.'
c23c AC2: 'One metrics evaluation does not rerun the same external analyzer once per registered metric.'
findings.finding: 0734's _analyzer_spec calls scc_loc.analyze directly (for LOC/size), while c23c introduces a separate composition seam that must also invoke scc (to avoid re-running it). The dedup guarantee in c23c ('one metrics evaluation does not rerun the same external analyzer once per registered metric') is ambiguous when two independent invocation paths exist — 0734's direct scc call and c23c's composition layer. The interaction between these two paths is unspecified, making the dedup guarantee unverifiable.
findings.location: child c23c-c32b-982c-4196
scenarios: c23c wraps all three analyzers in a cached composition call, but 0734's _analyzer_spec bypasses it by calling scc_loc.analyze directly, causing scc to run twice per metrics evaluation (once for each registered size metric, once for the composition).
A developer implementing c23c does not realize 0734 already calls scc directly and adds another scc call in the composition layer.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) have 0734's _analyzer_spec use the same cached/composed invocation that c23c defines (making c23c a prerequisite for 0734's final form), or (b) explicitly document in c23c that scc is called once for LOC (via 0734's direct path) and not re-invoked in the composition layer when only LOC is needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve c23c's file ambiguity: specify whether complexity/duplication MetricSpecs land in git_metrics.py (alongside size specs) or in a new dedicated module (e.g. complexity_metrics.py), and ensure it does not conflict with 0734's edits to the same file.
evidence: 0734 Files: 'src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py (add _analyzer_spec + reworked fns; remove hardcodes + cap_change_events), src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py (ctx carries the [code_health] config).'

c23c 'Likely files: src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py or a focused analyzer-composition module, src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py / registry integration seam, src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py context cache.'
c23c uses 'or' for its primary file target ('analyzer.py or a focused analyzer-composition module'), leaving the ownership unresolved.
findings.finding: c23c and 0734 both list src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py and src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py as 'likely files,' but c23c does not specify whether new complexity/duplication MetricSpecs are registered in git_metrics.py (alongside 0734's size specs) or in a new dedicated module. This undefined ownership creates a merge conflict risk and leaves the registry integration surface ambiguous.
findings.location: child c23c-c32b-982c-4196
scenarios: Two implementers both edit git_metrics.py in parallel (0734 for size, c23c for complexity/duplication) and produce conflicting register() call sequences or import blocks.
The registry ends up with complexity/duplication specs in an unexpected location, breaking the import chain that a532's tests depend on.
findings.suggested_fix: Define in c23c: 'New MetricSpecs for complexity and duplication are registered in a new src/rebar/metrics/structural_metrics.py (not git_metrics.py) to avoid merge conflicts with 0734; git_metrics.py is read-only for c23c.'

evidence: Child a9dd title: 'Docs: the [metrics] extra, [code_health] config, adapter set, and the fallback contract'
Plan AC item 6: 'Docs (reuse-surface, user-guide) describe the extra, config, and the fallback contract.' — 'adapter set' is absent with no stated rationale for the omission.
findings.finding: The plan's docs AC (item 6) silently omits 'adapter set' documentation that is explicitly required by child a9dd's scope.
scenarios: A consumer reading the plan's AC to write docs would not know to document which adapter/tool is used per language, leaving users without guidance on how to select or configure the scc/lizard/jscpd adapters.
A reviewer signing off on AC item 6 would accept docs that skip adapter-set documentation, silently violating the intent expressed in the child ticket.

findings.finding: Parent AC1 requires code_health metrics computed via the full analyzer stack (scc/lizard/jscpd), but only scc is wired into the registered code_health metric specs by any child. Children 21fb (lizard/complexity) and 3ba0 (jscpd/duplication) build adapters that are never wired into the code_health metric lens — no child defines metric specs or adapter calls for complexity or duplication data in the code_health output.
findings.location: child 0734-ceae-be55-4fa7 (Mechanism section); child 21fb-f4cd-3eaa-4b60; child 3ba0-9350-8337-429a
findings.suggested_fix: Either extend 0734's mechanism to add complexity/duplication fns over AnalyzerResult.complexity and AnalyzerResult.duplication (calling all three analyzers in _analyzer_spec or a composite analyze() call), or create a new child ticket that wires 21fb and 3ba0 outputs into registered MetricSpec entries, ensuring all three tools contribute to the code_health lens output.

findings.finding: The plan states 'Package tools + analyzer under an optional [metrics] extra' implying `pip install rebar[metrics]` delivers scc, lizard, and jscpd. However, scc is a Go binary (installable via `go install` or platform package managers, not pip) and jscpd is an npm package (installable via `npm install -g jscpd` or `npx jscpd`). Only lizard is pip-installable. The packaging mechanism is broken for two of the three tools: a `[metrics]` extra can deliver lizard as a Python dep, but scc and jscpd must be documented as system-level prerequisites (like `git` already is), not pip deps. If implemented as written, a user who runs `pip install rebar[metrics]` will still be missing scc and jscpd with no clear error at install time.
findings.location: Design section: 'Package tools + analyzer under an optional [metrics] extra; core install stays dependency-free'; Recommended default stack section (scc/jscpd descriptions)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Design section add: 'The [metrics] pip extra delivers lizard (Python) and the adapter/interface code. scc and jscpd are system-level CLIs — document them as prerequisites (e.g. `brew install scc` / `npm install -g jscpd`) alongside `git`, and ensure the Unavailable reason for each missing tool names its install command.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts that scc (a Go binary) and jscpd (a Node.js package) can be shipped as part of `pip install rebar[metrics]`, but neither can be declared as a Python package dependency in pyproject.toml `[project.optional-dependencies]`. The maintenance runbook confirms jscpd is used as `npx jscpd` (an npm precondition), not a pip dep. AC#2 ('analyzer + tools live behind `pip install rebar[metrics]`') is therefore technically infeasible as written for two of the three tools.
findings.location: AC#2 ('Zero new HARD deps in rebar core; analyzer + tools live behind `pip install rebar[metrics]`') and Recommended stack section ('scc (MIT, Go)' / 'jscpd (MIT)')
findings.suggested_fix: Restate AC#2 as: 'The `[metrics]` extra installs lizard; scc and jscpd are documented external prerequisites (Go binary / npx). Core-only install degrades to Unavailable with a clear message naming which tool is missing.' Add an installation guide section for non-Python tools.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
